events.text: The patient is a 48-year-old female named Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo. She is of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent. She works as a high school biology teacher. She is a non-smoker with no significant prior chemical or radiation exposures. She has a history of hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and mild hypertension. She is married with two adult children.

At age 48, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 5-week history of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, easy bruising, and spontaneous gingival bleeding while brushing her teeth. She also reported night sweats and a 7-pound unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. On physical examination, she was pale and mildly diaphoretic. Splenomegaly was palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin. Multiple petechiae were noted on bilateral lower extremities and soft palate. Active gingival oozing was observed. Initial CBC revealed WBC 87,400/uL, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, platelet count 28,000/uL. Peripheral blood smear demonstrated circulating blasts estimated at 42%. She was referred emergently to the hematology-oncology service and admitted to the hospital.

At age 48, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast performed at time of initial presentation, which showed splenomegaly measuring 16.4 cm in craniocaudal dimension with homogeneous parenchyma. No lymphadenopathy was identified above or below the diaphragm. No discrete hepatic lesions. Mild hepatomegaly with a liver span of 17.1 cm. No pleural effusions. No pericardial effusion. No pulmonary infiltrates or masses. Findings were consistent with hematologic malignancy. No evidence of extramedullary leukemic involvement (e.g., chloroma or myeloid sarcoma). Cancer status: cancer present, no distant metastatic solid organ involvement.

At age 48, the patient had a bone marrow biopsy and aspirate performed. The biopsy showed a hypercellular marrow (estimated cellularity 95%) with replacement of normal hematopoietic elements by sheets of myeloblasts. The aspirate differential count revealed 74% myeloblasts. Blasts were large with irregular nuclear contours, prominent nucleoli, and moderate pale cytoplasm with Auer rods identified in a subset. Flow cytometry immunophenotyping demonstrated a blast population positive for CD34, CD117 (c-Kit), CD13, CD33, CD123, HLA-DR, and MPO; negative for CD19, CD20, CD3, CD56, and TdT. Cytogenetics (conventional karyotype): 46,XX,+8[12]/46,XX[8] -- trisomy 8 in 12 of 20 metaphases, representing a clonal abnormality. FISH panel negative for t(8;21), inv(16)/t(16;16), t(15;17), and 11q23 rearrangements. Diagnosis: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), NOS (myeloblastic without maturation by FAB M1 features), with trisomy 8 as a sole cytogenetic abnormality. ELN 2022 risk classification: Intermediate (pending molecular). FLT3-ITD allelic ratio result pending.

At age 48 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) with FLT3-ITD mutation (high allelic ratio). AJCC staging not applicable. Summary stage: not applicable for leukemia (systemic/disseminated hematologic malignancy). Primary site: bone marrow/blood (hematopoietic system). ICD-O-3 site code: C42.1 (bone marrow). Histology: Acute myeloid leukemia, NOS; ICD-O-3 morphology code 9861/3. FAB classification: M1 (myeloblastic without maturation). Blast percentage at diagnosis: 74% by bone marrow aspirate differential. Peripheral blood blast percentage: approximately 42% by manual differential. WHO 2022 classification: AML, NOS. Cytogenetics: 46,XX,+8[12]/46,XX[8] (trisomy 8, intermediate risk cytogenetic abnormality). ELN 2022 risk: Adverse (reclassified from Intermediate after FLT3-ITD allelic ratio confirmed high at 0.91). FLT3-ITD allelic ratio: 0.91 (high allelic ratio, adverse prognosis). NPM1 mutation status: NEGATIVE (wild-type). CEBPA mutation status: negative (no biallelic CEBPA). IDH1/IDH2: negative. Initial presentation: leukocytosis (WBC 87,400/uL), severe anemia (Hgb 7.2 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (platelets 28,000/uL), active gingival bleeding, splenomegaly. Confirmation: Positive histology with flow cytometry immunophenotyping and molecular studies.

At age 48 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for acute myeloid leukemia based on a specimen obtained from bone marrow aspirate at diagnosis. The assay covered 468 genes (comprehensive hematologic malignancy panel). Clinically actionable findings: FLT3 p.ITD insertion (exon 14, 33-base pair tandem duplication), variant allele frequency (VAF) 48.2%, allelic ratio 0.91 (HIGH -- adverse prognosis per ELN 2022); DNMT3A p.R882H mutation, VAF 49.8% (presumed founder clone, associated with clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential/CHIP-origin leukemia); WT1 p.R462W mutation, VAF 31.4% (secondary mutation, associated with disease and potential MRD marker). Co-mutations and additional findings: NRAS p.Q61H mutation, VAF 18.7% (subclonal RAS pathway activation); TET2 p.Q1299* nonsense mutation, VAF 52.1% (likely founder/co-occurring); KDM6A p.S1025fs frameshift mutation, VAF 44.3%; STAG2 p.E253* nonsense mutation, VAF 21.6% (cohesion complex, subclonal); BCOR p.G1522fs frameshift mutation, VAF 14.2% (subclonal). Copy number alterations: Gain of chromosome 8 (consistent with cytogenetic trisomy 8). No copy number alterations detected in MYC, TP53, CDK6, RUNX1, or ETV6. Fusions: No clinically significant gene fusions detected (CBFB-MYH11, RUNX1-RUNX1T1, PML-RARA, KMT2A rearrangements all negative by RNA fusion panel). Notably ABSENT (as expected for FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio leukemia): NPM1 mutation negative; CEBPA mutation negative; KRAS mutation negative; IDH1/IDH2 negative; TP53 negative. Tumor mutational burden: 4.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite instability: stable. Summary: High allelic ratio FLT3-ITD AML with DNMT3A R882H (likely CHIP-origin founder), TET2, WT1, NRAS, KDM6A, STAG2, and BCOR co-mutations. NPM1 wild-type. ELN 2022 Adverse risk.

At age 48 years, the patient received induction chemotherapy with curative intent using the 7+3 regimen consisting of cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous intravenous infusion on days 1 through 7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 intravenous push on days 1, 2, and 3, with the addition of midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days 8 through 21 (FLT3 inhibitor per RATIFY protocol). The patient was hospitalized for induction. At the start of induction, her ECOG performance status was 1. She also received allopurinol 300 mg daily for tumor lysis prophylaxis, ciprofloxacin and fluconazole as antimicrobial prophylaxis, and acyclovir for viral prophylaxis. Transfusion support was provided as needed to maintain hemoglobin above 8 g/dL and platelets above 10,000/uL (or 20,000/uL with active bleeding).

At age 48, during induction chemotherapy (days 10-18), the patient developed febrile neutropenia with an absolute neutrophil count nadir of 0/uL and a single fever of 38.7 degrees Celsius. Blood cultures grew Escherichia coli (sensitive to ciprofloxacin) from one of two blood culture bottles, consistent with bacteremia. Ciprofloxacin was upgraded to cefepime 2g IV every 8 hours. She defervesced within 48 hours. No septic shock or end-organ dysfunction. This was a Grade 3 adverse event (febrile neutropenia with bacteremia). She also developed Grade 2 mucositis (painful oral ulcerations requiring opioid analgesics) that resolved by day 21. Grade 1 nausea was managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine. No clinical or laboratory evidence of tumor lysis syndrome.

At age 48, on day 14 of induction, the patient developed symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL) and received 2 units of packed red blood cells with appropriate hematologic response (post-transfusion Hgb 8.9 g/dL). She required a total of 3 platelet transfusions during induction nadir (platelet counts consistently below 10,000/uL). No intracranial hemorrhage or other major bleeding events occurred beyond the pre-treatment gingival bleeding. Cardiac monitoring showed no significant QTc prolongation on midostaurin (baseline QTc 412 ms, maximum QTc on treatment 438 ms).

At age 48, approximately 35 days after initiation of induction chemotherapy (day 35 bone marrow biopsy), the patient had a bone marrow assessment performed to evaluate response. The bone marrow biopsy showed a hypocellular marrow (estimated cellularity 10-15%) with trilineage hematopoiesis and no morphologic evidence of residual leukemia. Myeloblasts estimated at less than 3% by morphology. Flow cytometry multi-parameter MRD assay (10-color panel, sensitivity 0.01%) was NEGATIVE -- no aberrant blast immunophenotype detected. Peripheral blood CBC at day 35: WBC 3,200/uL with ANC 1,850/uL, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelets 112,000/uL. Molecular MRD by FLT3-ITD PCR: not detected (below limit of detection, sensitivity 10^-4). WT1 quantitative PCR: WT1 transcript level 26 copies/10^4 ABL1 copies (normal range less than 50). Conclusion: COMPLETE REMISSION (CR) with MRD negativity by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR after first induction. ELN 2022 CR criteria met.

At age 48, day 35 post-induction assessment. The patient achieved complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity after a single course of 7+3 plus midostaurin. She was seen in the outpatient hematology-oncology clinic. She was recovering well, with energy levels improving. Her ECOG performance status was 1. She was tolerating a soft diet and had resumed most activities of daily living. Physical examination: no pallor, no petechiae, no active bleeding. Splenomegaly had resolved (spleen not palpable). Oral mucosa was healing. Lymph nodes: no appreciable lymphadenopathy. Discussion regarding consolidation and transplant options initiated. Given her age (48), high-risk disease (ELN adverse: FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio with NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8, complex co-mutation profile including DNMT3A R882H and TET2), and absence of significant comorbidities, the multidisciplinary leukemia team strongly recommended proceeding to allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) in CR1. HLA typing was sent at diagnosis and results were available. She was referred to the transplant team for evaluation. Cancer status: CR, MRD negative.

At age 48, approximately 6 weeks after completing induction chemotherapy, a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed as part of pre-transplant workup. Findings showed interval improvement in splenomegaly (now 10.8 cm, previously 16.4 cm). No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. No evidence of myeloid sarcoma or extramedullary disease. Normal cardiac silhouette. No pleural or pericardial effusion. Cancer status: no cancer detected on imaging (CR documented by bone marrow biopsy). Echocardiogram performed concurrently as part of transplant workup: left ventricular ejection fraction 62%, no significant valvular disease, no wall motion abnormalities. Pulmonary function tests: FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 84% predicted (both within acceptable range for transplant).

At age 48, approximately 8 weeks after CR1 was documented, the patient was evaluated by the allogeneic stem cell transplant team. Transplant consultation note: The patient is a 48-year-old female with ELN adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8, DNMT3A R882H) in CR1 with MRD negativity. She is a candidate for reduced-intensity conditioning (RIC) or myeloablative conditioning (MAC) allo-HSCT. HLA typing was performed: Patient is HLA-A*02:01, A*24:02, B*07:02, B*44:03, C*07:02, C*16:01, DRB1*01:01, DRB1*15:01, DQB1*05:01, DQB1*06:02 (10/10 allele typed). Bone marrow registry search identified a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD), a 34-year-old male CMV-seropositive donor. Patient CMV serostatus: positive (CMV IgG positive). KPS score 90%. ECOG PS 1. Hematopoietic cell transplantation comorbidity index (HCT-CI): 1 (hypothyroidism). Decision: Proceed with myeloablative conditioning allo-HSCT from 10/10 MUD with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4) conditioning. GVHD prophylaxis: tacrolimus plus methotrexate. No additional consolidation chemotherapy prior to transplant given adequate marrow recovery and donor availability. Cancer present: no (in CR1, MRD negative).

At age 48 years, the patient received myeloablative conditioning chemotherapy for allogeneic stem cell transplantation consisting of fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day intravenously on days -6 through -3 (total 4 days) and busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day intravenously on days -6 through -3 (4 days, targeting area-under-the-curve of 4800-6000 micromol*min/L based on pharmacokinetic monitoring, actual cumulative busulfan exposure within therapeutic range after PK dose adjustment). Busulfan therapeutic drug monitoring was performed after the first dose; dose was adjusted from 3.2 mg/kg/day to 3.1 mg/kg/day for doses 2-4 to achieve target AUC. GVHD prophylaxis: tacrolimus target level 10-15 ng/mL starting day -3, and short-course methotrexate 15 mg/m2 IV on day +1 and 10 mg/m2 IV on days +3 and +6. Antimicrobial prophylaxis: acyclovir, fluconazole, and levofloxacin. CMV prophylaxis: letermovir 480 mg orally daily starting day +1 through day +100 (given CMV D+/R+ status).

At age 48 years, the patient received allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell infusion (day 0) from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor. Total CD34+ cell dose infused: 6.2 x 10^6 CD34+ cells/kg recipient body weight (adequate graft). CD3+ T-cell dose: 3.1 x 10^8 cells/kg. The infusion was well-tolerated. The patient received diphenhydramine and hydrocortisone pre-medication. No acute reactions during infusion. The patient was admitted to the hematopoietic stem cell transplant unit for the peri-transplant period.

At age 48, during the early post-transplant period (days +1 through +20), the patient developed expected aplasia-related complications. She developed Grade 3 mucositis (severe oral and esophageal mucositis requiring IV narcotic analgesia and total parenteral nutrition from days +7 through +16). She developed febrile neutropenia on day +8 (temperature 38.9 degrees Celsius, ANC 0/uL). Blood cultures were negative. Empiric antifungal coverage was escalated to micafungin on day +12 when she remained febrile despite broad-spectrum antibiotics; CT chest on day +13 showed no infiltrates or nodular lesions. She defervesced by day +15. Grade 2 diarrhea occurred from days +8 to +14, attributed to conditioning toxicity and methotrexate. She received aggressive IV fluid support. Busulfan-related hepatotoxicity: mild, Grade 1 transaminase elevation (AST peak 78 U/L, ALT peak 92 U/L) without hyperbilirubinemia; no sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD). Tacrolimus levels were therapeutic throughout.

At age 48, on day +21 post-transplant, the patient achieved neutrophil engraftment (ANC greater than 500/uL for two consecutive days, first documented on day +20). Platelet engraftment (platelet count greater than 20,000/uL without transfusion support for two consecutive days) occurred on day +24. Peripheral blood chimerism assessment on day +21: overall donor chimerism 97%, CD3+ T-cell chimerism 89%, CD33+ myeloid chimerism 99%. Findings consistent with robust early engraftment. CBC on day +21: WBC 1,900/uL, ANC 820/uL, hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL (transfusion-dependent), platelets 34,000/uL. The patient was discharged to outpatient transplant follow-up on day +26 in good condition. ECOG PS 2 (decreased due to post-transplant fatigue and deconditioning). No signs of acute GVHD at time of discharge. Cancer status: presumed remission (formal MRD testing planned at day +30).

At age 48, on day +30 post-transplant, bone marrow biopsy and aspirate were performed for day +30 engraftment and disease assessment. Biopsy: moderately cellular marrow (cellularity 40-50%) with trilineage hematopoiesis and no morphologic evidence of residual AML. Myeloblasts less than 3% by morphology. Flow cytometry MRD (10-color, sensitivity 0.01%): NEGATIVE -- no aberrant blast immunophenotype detected. FLT3-ITD PCR by bone marrow: not detected (below sensitivity threshold 10^-4). WT1 quantitative PCR: 18 copies/10^4 ABL1 copies (normal). Peripheral blood STR chimerism (day +30): overall chimerism 99.4% donor, CD3+ T-cell chimerism 95.2% donor, CD33+ myeloid chimerism 100% donor. Conclusion: Complete remission maintained, MRD negative, full donor chimerism in myeloid compartment. No evidence of graft failure or mixed chimerism concerning for relapse. Cancer status: MRD-negative complete remission.

At age 48, between days +45 and +90 post-transplant, the patient developed acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving the skin and gastrointestinal tract. Skin: macular-papular rash involving approximately 30% body surface area (Stage 2 skin GVHD). Gastrointestinal: Grade 2 diarrhea (500-1000 mL/day loose stool, Stage 1 GI GVHD). Overall acute GVHD grading: Grade II (moderate) by Glucksberg criteria. Treatment: systemic methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day initiated at day +47. Tacrolimus level was optimized (target 12-15 ng/mL). Skin rash improved significantly within 10 days of steroid initiation. GI symptoms resolved by day +65. Steroids were tapered over 6 weeks by 10% per week starting at day +55 once symptoms were controlled. The patient responded well to initial steroid therapy with no requirement for second-line immunosuppression. The patient was maintained on prophylactic trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (for PCP), acyclovir, and letermovir through day +100.

At age 48, on day +90 post-transplant, a surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed. Findings: No evidence of lymphadenopathy. No hepatic or splenic lesions. Spleen is normal in size (10.2 cm). Mild ground-glass opacities in the right lower lobe (1.2 cm area), appearing post-infectious or inflammatory in nature; no consolidation. No pleural effusion. No pericardial effusion. Compared to prior imaging, there is complete resolution of the previously noted hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. The mild pulmonary opacity was monitored with repeat imaging. Cancer status: no cancer detected on imaging. No evidence of extramedullary relapse or myeloid sarcoma.

At age 48, at approximately 4 months (day +120) post-transplant, the patient was seen in the transplant outpatient clinic. She reported significant improvement in energy levels, though persistent mild fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance compared to her pre-diagnosis baseline. She noted new-onset dry eyes and mild dry mouth over the preceding 3-4 weeks, as well as skin tightening on her forearms. Physical examination revealed subtle skin xerosis on bilateral forearms without frank sclerodermatous changes. Schirmer's test: 6 mm/5 minutes in the right eye, 5 mm/5 minutes in the left eye (below normal, suggestive of sicca features). Oral examination: mild mucosal erythema and reduced salivary pooling. These findings are consistent with early chronic GVHD (cGVHD) involving skin, eyes, and mouth. NIH 2014 Chronic GVHD Global Severity Score: MILD (1-2 organs involved, no severe involvement). CBC: WBC 5,800/uL, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, platelets 187,000/uL. Tacrolimus taper to continue. Topical steroids for skin initiated. Artificial tears and cyclosporine ophthalmic emulsion prescribed. ECOG PS 1. Cancer status: in remission, cancer absent.

At age 48, between months 4 and 8 post-transplant, the patient was managed for mild chronic GVHD. The chronic GVHD manifestations remained stable and mild, involving skin (xerosis, mild erythema, no scleroderma), eyes (sicca features), and mouth (mild erythema, reduced saliva). NIH Global Severity: mild throughout. Management consisted of topical corticosteroid cream (triamcinolone 0.1%) for skin, cyclosporine ophthalmic emulsion 0.05% twice daily for eyes, artificial tears, and continuation of low-dose systemic tacrolimus (dose tapered to 0.5 mg twice daily by month 6 with trough levels 5-8 ng/mL). No systemic corticosteroids required for chronic GVHD management. The patient also developed Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral distal tingling in toes), felt to be tacrolimus-related, which was monitored. Tacrolimus was gradually tapered with plan to discontinue at month 12 if cGVHD remained stable. PCP prophylaxis, acyclovir, and antifungal prophylaxis continued through month 6 and then narrowed thereafter per institutional transplant protocol.

At age 48-49, at 6 months post-transplant, a surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed. All findings were within normal limits. No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. Normal spleen size (9.8 cm). The previously noted right lower lobe ground-glass opacity had completely resolved. No new pulmonary findings. No ascites or pleural effusions. Cancer status: no cancer detected. No evidence of leukemic relapse or extramedullary AML. Normal appearing post-transplant imaging.

At age 49, at 6 months post-transplant (day +180), bone marrow biopsy and aspirate were performed for surveillance. Biopsy: normocellular marrow (cellularity 50-55%) with complete trilineage hematopoiesis including adequate megakaryopoiesis. No morphologic evidence of AML. Myeloblasts less than 2% by morphology. Flow cytometry MRD (sensitivity 0.01%): NEGATIVE. FLT3-ITD PCR by peripheral blood: not detected. WT1 quantitative PCR (peripheral blood): 11 copies/10^4 ABL1 copies (normal). Peripheral blood STR chimerism (day +180): overall chimerism 99.8% donor, CD3+ T-cell chimerism 98.1% donor, CD33+ myeloid chimerism 100% donor. Conclusion: Sustained complete remission with MRD negativity and full donor chimerism at 6 months. Excellent post-transplant response. Cancer status: MRD-negative CR2/ongoing CR1, full donor engraftment.

At age 49, at approximately 9 months post-transplant, the patient was seen in the transplant and leukemia clinics for co-management. Her chronic GVHD manifestations (skin, eyes, mouth) continued to improve with ongoing topical management. Tacrolimus was successfully tapered to 0.5 mg once daily. The peripheral neuropathy in her toes had partially improved. She reported returning to part-time work as a teacher at 7 months post-transplant. CBC was stable: WBC 6,400/uL, hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, platelets 224,000/uL. She was transitioned from TMP-SMX daily to three-times-weekly dosing for ongoing PCP prophylaxis. A bone marrow biopsy was deferred given the excellent 6-month result and ongoing clinical stability; peripheral blood MRD testing (FLT3-ITD PCR and chimerism) was performed and was reassuring. She continued on acyclovir and was off letermovir (discontinued at day +100 per protocol). No CMV reactivation events had occurred. She was counseled about long-term survivorship issues including secondary malignancy screening, bone health (DEXA scan ordered given steroid exposure), cardiovascular risk, and monitoring for hypothyroidism (pre-existing). Cancer status: in sustained complete remission, no evidence of disease.

At age 49, at 12 months post-transplant, a surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed as part of the annual post-transplant assessment. Findings: No evidence of lymphadenopathy above or below the diaphragm. Normal liver size and echogenicity. Normal spleen size (9.5 cm). No pulmonary nodules, masses, or infiltrates. No pleural or pericardial effusion. No ascites. No evidence of myeloid sarcoma, chloroma, or extramedullary leukemic involvement. Cancer status: no cancer detected. Complete radiographic response maintained at 12 months post-transplant.

At age 49, at 12 months post-transplant (day +365), bone marrow biopsy and aspirate were performed for annual surveillance assessment. Biopsy: normocellular marrow (cellularity 45-50%) with complete, morphologically normal trilineage hematopoiesis. No morphologic evidence of AML. Myeloblasts less than 2%. Flow cytometry MRD (10-color, sensitivity 0.01%): NEGATIVE. FLT3-ITD PCR: not detected. WT1 quantitative PCR: 9 copies/10^4 ABL1 copies (normal). DNMT3A R882H allele-specific PCR (peripheral blood): not detected (below limit of detection). STR chimerism: overall 99.9% donor, CD3+ T-cell 99.2% donor, CD33+ myeloid 100% donor. Conclusion: Complete remission with sustained MRD negativity and full donor chimerism at 12 months. Clinically meaningful cure trajectory. All molecular markers of original AML clone are undetectable. Cancer status: MRD-negative complete remission at 1-year post-transplant.

At age 49, at 12-month post-transplant survivorship visit, the patient was seen in combined transplant survivorship and leukemia clinics. She has successfully returned to full-time teaching as of 10 months post-transplant. Chronic GVHD manifestations are resolved or near-resolved: skin is normal without xerosis or erythema, eye symptoms have improved significantly (Schirmer's test now 9 mm/5 minutes bilaterally), oral mucosa is normal with adequate salivation. Tacrolimus was discontinued at 11.5 months post-transplant with no GVHD flare. The peripheral neuropathy has fully resolved after tacrolimus discontinuation. NIH chronic GVHD Global Severity: none (resolved). She had a DEXA scan demonstrating mild osteopenia (T-score -1.4 lumbar spine, -1.2 femoral neck); calcium and vitamin D supplementation initiated. Thyroid function tests within normal limits on stable levothyroxine dose. Blood pressure well-controlled. The patient is counseled about AML relapse risk (greatest in the first 2 years post-transplant, particularly for FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio disease), the importance of continued surveillance, and transition to primary care for long-term survivorship management. Her overall clinical course is excellent: she achieved CR1 MRD-negative status after first induction, underwent MUD allo-HSCT without major life-threatening complications, had only mild chronic GVHD (now resolved), and maintains full donor chimerism with undetectable molecular disease at 12 months. ECOG PS 0. Cancer status: complete remission, MRD negative, no evidence of disease at 12 months post-transplant. Prognosis: favorable given sustained MRD negativity and full donor chimerism.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY-ONCOLOGY INPATIENT CONSULT NOTE

ATTENDING: [Hematology-Oncology]
CONSULT SERVICE: Hematology-Oncology
REQUESTING SERVICE: Internal Medicine / Primary Care
REASON FOR CONSULT: Acute leukocytosis with circulating blasts, thrombocytopenia, anemia -- urgent hematologic evaluation and management

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female presenting with a 5-week history of worsening fatigue, exertional shortness of breath, easy bruising, and bleeding gums. She was found to have a WBC of 87,400/uL with an estimated 42% circulating blasts on peripheral smear and was referred emergently for hematology-oncology evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female biology teacher with a background of well-controlled hypothyroidism and mild hypertension who presents with a several-week history of a progressive systemic illness that has substantially impaired her functioning. She reports that approximately 5 weeks ago she began noticing unusually severe fatigue -- described as bone-deep exhaustion -- which she initially attributed to occupational stress near the end of the school term. Over the subsequent weeks, she developed increasing shortness of breath with exertion, noting that she becomes winded climbing a single flight of stairs, an activity she previously performed without difficulty. She has noted easy bruising over minor trauma sites, as well as spontaneous bleeding from her gums while brushing her teeth, which has been persistent and difficult to control. She reports drenching night sweats requiring a change of bedclothes on several occasions per week. She has had an unintentional weight loss of approximately 7 pounds over the past 6 weeks without dietary modification and without obvious explanation.

She presented to her primary care physician today for evaluation. On examination there, she was found to be pale and mildly diaphoretic. Splenomegaly was noted on palpation, approximately 3 cm below the left costal margin. Multiple petechiae were observed on the bilateral lower extremities and soft palate. There was active oozing from the gingival margins. A CBC obtained urgently at the primary care office revealed a WBC of 87,400/uL, hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL, and platelet count of 28,000/uL. Peripheral blood smear was reviewed and demonstrated circulating blasts estimated at approximately 42% of white cell differential. She was referred directly to our hematology-oncology service and has been admitted for urgent evaluation.

She denies headache, visual changes, confusion, focal neurologic symptoms, chest pain, hemoptysis, hematuria, melena, or hematochezia. She has not had fevers prior to today's presentation. She denies prior episodes of similar symptoms. She has no known prior hematologic disorders. She is a non-smoker and reports no significant prior chemical or occupational exposures. She has not been taking NSAIDs or anticoagulants.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypothyroidism -- managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH last checked approximately 8 months ago, within normal limits per patient report
2. Mild hypertension -- previously treated with lifestyle modification; recently started on low-dose antihypertensive (see medications)
3. No prior malignancy
4. No prior hematologic disorders
5. No prior radiation therapy or chemotherapy exposure
6. Spontaneous vaginal delivery x2, no obstetric complications

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Appendectomy, age 22 (uncomplicated)
- No other prior surgeries

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo works full-time as a high school biology teacher. She is married with two adult children. She is a lifelong non-smoker. She reports occasional social alcohol use (1-2 drinks per week, less than prior to symptom onset). She denies illicit drug use. She denies recreational cannabis use. She denies any occupational or environmental chemical exposures of significance (no pesticide, benzene, or radiation exposure identified). She was born outside the United States; country of origin not specified in current records. She lives with her husband in the area. She has a strong support system at home.

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Mother: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension; alive
- Father: Deceased, cause unknown to patient
- One brother: alive, no known malignancy
- No known family history of leukemia, lymphoma, myelodysplastic syndrome, or other hematologic malignancy
- No known family history of inherited bone marrow failure syndromes (Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita, Diamond-Blackfan anemia)
- No known family history of breast, ovarian, or colon cancer

---

ALLERGIES:
- Penicillin: reported rash (nature and timing unclear; documented as allergy in primary care records)
- No other known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

---

MEDICATIONS (on admission):
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally every morning on an empty stomach
2. Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily (for hypertension)
3. Multivitamin daily
4. No anticoagulants, no antiplatelets, no NSAIDs
5. No prior immunosuppressants or chemotherapy

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive for: profound fatigue, exertional dyspnea, easy bruising, spontaneous gingival bleeding, night sweats, unintentional weight loss (~7 lbs/6 weeks), pallor noted by family.
Negative for: fever (prior to today), chills, headache, visual disturbances, confusion, altered mental status, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, hematuria, melena, hematochezia, hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, focal neurologic deficits, bone pain, joint pain, rash, oral ulcers (aside from bleeding gums), lymph node swelling noted by patient.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: Temperature 37.1°C, heart rate 102 bpm (sinus tachycardia), blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: 67 kg. Height: 163 cm. BMI: 25.2 kg/m².

General: Pale-appearing female in mild distress, tired-appearing, in no acute respiratory distress. Alert and oriented x3. Somewhat anxious.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female with newly diagnosed ELN adverse-risk AML -- FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio (0.91), NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8, with DNMT3A R882H founder mutation and multiple co-mutations on NGS -- who has achieved a complete remission with MRD negativity after a single course of 7+3 plus midostaurin induction chemotherapy. This is an excellent early result, though her underlying disease biology remains high-risk and the probability of relapse without definitive consolidation is substantial.

1. AML IN CR1, MRD-NEGATIVE -- PROCEEDING TO ALLOGENEIC STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION:

The day +35 marrow assessment confirms CR by all criteria: morphologic CR (blasts less than 5%), hematologic recovery (ANC greater than 1,000/uL, platelets greater than 100,000/uL), MRD negativity by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR, and WT1 transcript within normal range. This is an ideal outcome after first induction.

However, the multidisciplinary leukemia team -- including hematology/oncology, transplant, and pathology -- has discussed this case in our weekly tumor board. There is strong consensus that this patient requires consolidation with allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) in CR1. The rationale is compelling:
- FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio without NPM1 co-mutation confers a relapse rate in excess of 50-60% with chemotherapy alone even after MRD-negative CR1
- DNMT3A R882H co-mutation further worsens prognosis in this context and is associated with chemotherapy resistance and relapse
- Trisomy 8 and the complex co-mutation landscape (TET2, WT1, NRAS, STAG2, BCOR) add additional adverse prognostic weight
- She is 48 years old with an ECOG PS of 1, HCT-CI score of 1 (hypothyroidism), and no significant organ dysfunction -- she is an excellent transplant candidate
- The graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect from allo-HSCT offers the best available chance of durable disease control in this setting

HLA typing was sent at diagnosis. Results are now available. She is typed at 10 alleles (HLA-A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1). We have reviewed these results with the transplant team. A bone marrow registry search has been initiated through the Be The Match (NMDP) registry. She has been formally referred to our stem cell transplant service for consultation and pre-transplant evaluation.

We discussed the rationale for transplant at length with the patient and her husband today. She understands the risks and benefits of allo-HSCT, including graft-versus-host disease, infectious complications, engraftment failure, transplant-related mortality, and the risk of relapse post-transplant despite transplant. She was counseled that proceeding to transplant in CR1 with MRD negativity represents the most favorable time to undergo this procedure, and that waiting for relapse would substantially worsen outcomes. She expressed willingness to proceed.

No additional consolidation chemotherapy cycles are planned prior to transplantation. The decision to proceed directly from CR1 to transplant without interim HiDAC consolidation is supported by recent data and is consistent with our institutional approach for fit patients with an identified donor and adverse-risk AML -- avoiding additional toxicity while minimizing time off the marrow nadir.

Comprehensive NGS panel (468-gene hematologic malignancy panel) confirmed a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (VAF 48.2%, allelic ratio 0.91 -- adverse prognosis), along with the following co-mutations: DNMT3A R882H (VAF 49.8%, likely CHIP-origin founder clone), TET2 Q1299* (VAF 52.1%), WT1 R462W (VAF 31.4%, potential MRD marker), NRAS Q61H (VAF 18.7%, subclonal), KDM6A S1025fs (VAF 44.3%), STAG2 E253* (VAF 21.6%), and BCOR G1522fs (VAF 14.2%). NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, TP53, CEBPA, and KRAS were all negative. ELN 2022 risk classification: Adverse (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio with NPM1 wild-type, further compounded by DNMT3A R882H and the complex multi-hit co-mutation landscape).

She was treated with standard 7+3 induction: cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day CIV days 1-7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV push days 1-3, with midostaurin 50 mg PO BID added on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by Grade 3 febrile neutropenia with E. coli bacteremia (treated successfully with cefepime), Grade 2 mucositis requiring opioid analgesia, and expected transfusion dependence. She did not develop clinical tumor lysis syndrome. Cardiac monitoring showed no significant QTc prolongation with midostaurin (max QTc 438 ms).

Day +35 bone marrow biopsy showed a hypocellular marrow (10-15% cellularity) with trilineage hematopoiesis and no morphologic residual AML (blasts <3%). Multi-parameter flow cytometry MRD assay (10-color, sensitivity 0.01%) was negative. FLT3-ITD PCR was below the limit of detection (sensitivity 10^-4). She was seen in outpatient leukemia clinic at day 35 in good condition; ECOG PS 1. She was counseled regarding the strong recommendation for allo-HSCT consolidation given her adverse-risk disease profile, and she agreed to proceed with transplant evaluation. HLA typing was sent at the time of diagnosis.

Pre-transplant workup CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (performed approximately 6 weeks post-induction) showed interval improvement in splenomegaly (now 10.8 cm, previously 16.4 cm at diagnosis), with no lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions, and no evidence of myeloid sarcoma or extramedullary disease. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female with ELN 2022 Adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio 0.91, NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8, DNMT3A R882H, TET2, WT1, and multiple additional co-mutations) who achieved MRD-negative CR1 after a single cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin. She has recovered well from induction, with near-normalization of counts, resolution of splenomegaly, and ECOG PS 1 with KPS 90%.

She presents for transplant evaluation and is deemed a candidate for allo-HSCT in CR1.

1. TRANSPLANT INDICATION AND RATIONALE:
   The indication for allo-HSCT in CR1 is well-supported. Her adverse-risk disease features -- high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD in an NPM1 wild-type context, trisomy 8, and a complex co-mutation landscape including DNMT3A R882H and TET2 (often referred to as a "CHIP-origin" AML, known to carry inferior outcomes) -- place her at very high risk of relapse with chemotherapy alone. Multiple retrospective and prospective datasets, as well as NCCN and ELN guidelines, support allo-HSCT consolidation in CR1 for this molecular profile. Her MRD-negative status going into transplant is the most favorable prognostic factor we can bring to the procedure.

2. HCT COMORBIDITY INDEX AND FITNESS:
   HCT-CI: 1 (hypothyroidism, managed, no organ dysfunction). Cardiopulmonary function: acceptable for myeloablative conditioning. Renal and hepatic function: within normal limits. KPS 90%, ECOG PS 1. She is fit for myeloablative conditioning (MAC).

3. CONDITIONING REGIMEN:
   Given her age (48, within typical MAC range at most institutions), absence of major comorbidities, and the high-risk disease biology (where a stronger graft-versus-leukemia effect and deeper marrow ablation are desirable), we recommend MYELOABLATIVE CONDITIONING (MAC) with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4):
   - Fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day IV x 4 days (days -6 through -3)
   - Busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day IV x 4 days (days -6 through -3) with pharmacokinetic monitoring and dose adjustment to target AUC 4800-6000 micromol*min/L
   This regimen provides robust myeloablation while the pharmacokinetic monitoring of busulfan minimizes the risk of sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD) and excessive extramedullary toxicity.

4. DONOR:
   Proceed with 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (34-year-old male, CMV seropositive). This is an excellent match; single-antigen mismatch or less is the standard threshold. Peripheral blood stem cells (PBSC) will be requested as the graft source to optimize engraftment kinetics.

5. GRAFT-VERSUS-HOST DISEASE PROPHYLAXIS:
   Tacrolimus (target trough 10-15 ng/mL starting day -3) plus short-course methotrexate (MTX 15 mg/m2 IV day +1; 10 mg/m2 IV days +3 and +6) per our institutional standard for MUD transplant. Post-transplant cyclophosphamide was discussed but tacrolimus/MTX remains our standard for 10/10 MUD in the MAC setting at this center.

6. INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS:
   - Acyclovir: Continue through at least day +365 (standard herpes/VZV prophylaxis).
   - Letermovir 480 mg PO daily starting day +1 through day +100 (CMV prophylaxis, given D+/R+ status).
   - Fluconazole: Continue through engraftment; reassess based on clinical status.
   - Levofloxacin: Antibacterial prophylaxis during neutropenic period.
   - TMP-SMX: PCP prophylaxis to begin after engraftment.

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   Transfusion support as per institutional protocol. G-CSF post-transplant not routinely used with PBSC graft; will reassess based on engraftment kinetics. Nutritional consultation pre-admission. Social work referral completed (patient seen today; support resources provided). Psychology consultation will be offered.

8. ADDITIONAL CONSOLIDATION PRIOR TO TRANSPLANT:
   NOT recommended. Given adequate marrow recovery, donor availability, MRD-negative CR1 status, and no evidence of extramedullary disease, proceeding directly to transplant without additional consolidation is appropriate and avoids unnecessary toxicity and the risk of disease recurrence during inter-treatment interval.

9. REPRODUCTIVE COUNSELING:
   The patient is 48 years old. She has been counseled that allo-HSCT with myeloablative conditioning will result in permanent gonadal failure and infertility. She and her husband were seen together and she reports she has completed childbearing. She was offered formal reproductive endocrinology consultation and declined, stating she understood the permanent nature of treatment-related menopause. Hormone replacement therapy options were briefly reviewed.

10. TIMELINE:
    Target transplant admission in approximately 2-3 weeks pending donor mobilization scheduling and completion of remaining logistics. Dental clearance requested. Central venous access (tunneled catheter or PICC) to be placed prior to conditioning initiation.

The patient and her husband were present during this consultation. All aspects of the transplant procedure, including risks (engraftment failure, infections, acute and chronic GVHD, organ toxicity, secondary malignancy, relapse), benefits (graft-versus-leukemia effect, potential for cure), and alternatives (additional chemotherapy cycles without transplant, enrollment on clinical trial if available) were discussed in detail. The patient expressed understanding of the rationale for proceeding and wishes to go forward with allo-HSCT. She has an excellent support system and appropriate understanding of the extended recovery timeline.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with ELN Adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 WT, trisomy 8, complex co-mutation profile) who is now at day +120 post-myeloablative 10/10 MUD allo-HSCT, with full donor engraftment, sustained MRD-negative complete remission, and no evidence of leukemic relapse.

Her prior acute GVHD Grade II (skin and GI) was treated successfully with steroids and has fully resolved.

1. CHRONIC GVHD -- NEW DIAGNOSIS, MILD:
   These findings are most consistent with early chronic GVHD (cGVHD) with multiorgan involvement: skin (subtle sclerosis/thickening, bilateral forearms -- NIH skin score 1), eyes (sicca, NIH eye score 1), and mouth (mucosal erythema, reduced saliva -- NIH mouth score 1). By NIH 2014 Consensus Criteria, this is classified as mild chronic GVHD (1-3 organs involved, none with severe NIH score, no lung or esophageal involvement, no functional impairment). No overlap features of acute GVHD at this time.
   - Hold further tacrolimus taper for now; maintain current tacrolimus 0.5 mg BID with trough targeting 8-12 ng/mL.
   - Initiate topical triamcinolone 0.1% cream to bilateral dorsal forearms, apply twice daily. Counseled patient to apply after moisturizing and to limit application to areas of thickening. No systemic corticosteroids indicated at this time given mild overall severity.
   - Refer to ophthalmology for formal evaluation of sicca/dry eye syndrome. In the interim, prescribe cyclosporine ophthalmic emulsion 0.05% (Restasis) one drop in each eye twice daily for anti-inflammatory management of aqueous-deficient dry eye. Continue artificial tears as needed (preservative-free preferred given anticipated frequent use).
   - For oral cGVHD: dexamethasone oral rinse (0.5 mg/5 mL) swish and spit twice daily for buccal erythema. Encourage good oral hygiene and regular dental follow-up.
   - Systemic steroids are NOT indicated at this stage given mild NIH global severity. Will reassess at next visit.
   - Initiate formal NIH cGVHD organ-specific scoring at each subsequent visit for longitudinal tracking.
   - Continue PCP, antifungal, and antiviral prophylaxis given ongoing immunosuppression.

2. AML REMISSION SURVEILLANCE -- DAY +120:
   Leukemia remains in deep molecular remission. FLT3-ITD PCR undetectable in peripheral blood. Full donor myeloid chimerism confirmed. CBC counts within normal limits with appropriate recovery. No clinical concern for early relapse.

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo was initially diagnosed with AML approximately 14 months ago after presenting to her primary care physician with progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, spontaneous gingival bleeding, and night sweats. Her initial CBC revealed a white count of 87,400/uL with roughly 42% peripheral blasts, hemoglobin of 7.2, and platelets of 28,000. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed AML (FAB M1 morphology, 74% blasts by aspirate differential) with trisomy 8 on cytogenetics and subsequently identified high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (allelic ratio 0.91) on molecular profiling. NGS demonstrated a complex co-mutation profile including DNMT3A R882H (VAF 49.8%, presumed founder/CHIP-origin), TET2 Q1299*, WT1 R462W, NRAS Q61H, KDM6A, STAG2, and BCOR mutations. NPM1 was wild-type. ELN 2022 risk: Adverse.

She received induction chemotherapy with 7+3 (cytarabine plus daunorubicin) plus midostaurin (RATIFY protocol). Induction was complicated by E. coli bacteremia during febrile neutropenia, Grade 2 mucositis, and expected transfusion requirements, but no life-threatening complications. Day 35 marrow assessment confirmed complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by flow cytometry and FLT3-ITD PCR. Given her adverse-risk disease profile, she was referred to our transplant program and underwent myeloablative conditioning (fludarabine/busulfan, FluBu4, with PK-adjusted busulfan dosing) followed by peripheral blood stem cell infusion from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (CD34+ dose 6.2 x 10^6/kg). Post-transplant course was notable for Grade 3 mucositis, febrile neutropenia with negative cultures, and mild transaminase elevation without SOS/VOD. Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day +20, platelet engraftment by day +24.

Post-transplant course has been overall excellent. She developed Grade II acute GVHD (Stage 2 skin, Stage 1 GI) at approximately day +47, which responded well to methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day with steroid taper over 6 weeks -- no second-line therapy required. She subsequently developed mild chronic GVHD involving skin (xerosis), eyes (sicca features, Schirmer's 5-6 mm bilaterally), and mouth (mild mucosal erythema), managed with topical agents and tacrolimus continuation. Serial marrow assessments at day +30, day +180, and now day +365 have all been MRD-negative with full or near-full donor chimerism. Tacrolimus was discontinued approximately 2 weeks ago (at approximately 11.5 months post-transplant) without any GVHD flare. The peripheral neuropathy she had experienced on tacrolimus (bilateral distal toe tingling, Grade 1) has resolved since discontinuation.

Today she reports significant improvement in her overall quality of life. She has been back to full-time teaching since approximately month 10 post-transplant. She denies new rash, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, or dysphagia. Eye symptoms have substantially improved -- she is still using artificial tears intermittently but no longer finds them bothersome on a day-to-day basis. Dry mouth is largely resolved. She endorses mild residual fatigue at end of the school day, which she feels is continuing to improve. She denies bone pain, joint stiffness, shortness of breath, or chest discomfort. No fevers, no night sweats, no weight loss. She has been exercising lightly (30-minute walks, 4-5 days/week). Appetite is excellent. Sleep is adequate.

Today's visit also includes review of the day +365 surveillance bone marrow biopsy results (performed earlier this week), which are reviewed in detail below.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 wild-type, ELN Adverse risk) -- STATUS: COMPLETE REMISSION, MRD-NEGATIVE, 12 MONTHS POST-MUD ALLO-HSCT
   This is an outstanding result. Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo achieved CR1 with MRD negativity after a single cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin, proceeded to MUD allo-HSCT in CR1 with FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning, and has now maintained undetectable molecular disease with full donor chimerism at 12 months. All three measurable residual disease markers -- flow cytometry, FLT3-ITD PCR, and WT1 -- remain negative/normal. The DNMT3A R882H founder mutation (which is a CHIP-origin mutation and thus could in theory persist even in the absence of leukemic relapse) is also undetectable, which is a favorable sign, though interpretation of CHIP-level markers post-transplant requires caution. The most common relapse window for FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio AML is within the first 18-24 months post-transplant, so we will continue close surveillance. She is now at low probability of relapse given these results, but not zero.
   - Continue surveillance: peripheral blood chimerism and FLT3-ITD PCR every 3 months through month 24, then every 6 months through year 3
   - Next bone marrow biopsy planned at 18 months post-transplant (month 18) unless earlier clinical concern arises
   - Next combined clinic visit in 3 months
   - Patient counseled at length about symptoms of relapse (new fatigue, fevers, bleeding, weight loss) and instructed to contact clinic urgently if any of these develop

2. Chronic GVHD -- RESOLVED
   Her mild chronic GVHD (skin, eyes, mouth) has fully resolved. Tacrolimus was successfully discontinued at 11.5 months without any flare. Schirmer's test has normalized (9 mm bilaterally). Skin is entirely normal. Oral mucosa normal. NIH Global Chronic GVHD Severity Score: 0 (none). No ongoing immunosuppressive therapy at present.

   - Fungal prophylaxis discontinued per protocol
   - Repeat vaccination series to begin at month 12-18: influenza, pneumococcal, meningococcal, Tdap, and hepatitis vaccines per ASTCT post-transplant immunization schedule

BONE MARROW CORE BIOPSY (H&E, PAS, reticulin stains):

Cellularity: Markedly hypercellular, estimated at 90-95% (age-adjusted normal for a 48-year-old female: approximately 50-60%). The adipose tissue is nearly completely replaced.

Architecture: The normal hematopoietic architecture is effaced. There is diffuse, sheet-like replacement of the marrow space by a monotonous population of large immature cells morphologically consistent with myeloblasts. Residual scattered erythroid islands and rare dysplastic megakaryocytes are identified at the periphery of the biopsy core. The sinusoidal architecture is largely preserved.

Blast Morphology: The predominant cells are large, with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, irregular to round nuclear contours, finely dispersed to vesicular chromatin, and one to three prominent nucleoli. Moderate amounts of pale to lightly basophilic cytoplasm are present. On Wright-Giemsa stained aspirate smears, thin needle-like azurophilic cytoplasmic inclusions consistent with Auer rods are identified in a subset of the blast population (estimated 10-15% of blasts; single Auer rods per cell, no faggot cells identified). Granulopoiesis: Markedly suppressed. Only rare maturing granulocytic forms identified.

FLOW CYTOMETRY IMMUNOPHENOTYPING (10-color multiparameter flow, fresh aspirate):
Gating strategy: CD45 dim/low SSC gate used to identify blast population. An aberrant CD34+/CD117+ population constitutes approximately 72% of all nucleated marrow events.

Blast Population Immunophenotype:

  POSITIVE (dim expression): CD123, CD38 (partial/heterogeneous)
  NEGATIVE: CD19, CD20, CD22, CD79a, CD3, CD5, CD7, CD56, CD16, CD64, CD14, CD11b, CD15 (dim, heterogeneous - not diagnostic of monocytic differentiation), TdT, CD10

Interpretation: The aberrant blast immunophenotype is consistent with acute myeloid leukemia, myeloblastic type. The expression of CD34, CD117, CD13, CD33, HLA-DR, and MPO without lymphoid or monocytic markers supports myeloid lineage assignment. Absence of TdT and lymphoid antigens excludes acute lymphoblastic leukemia. The CD56-negative phenotype is noted. CD123 co-expression is consistent with myeloblastic AML and is not sufficient for plasmacytoid dendritic cell neoplasm (PDCN) in this context.

Number of metaphases analyzed: 20
Result: 46,XX,+8[12]/46,XX[8]

Eight metaphases showed a normal female karyotype, consistent with residual normal hematopoiesis. Trisomy 8 is a recurrent clonal abnormality in AML and myeloid neoplasms; as a sole abnormality, it is associated with intermediate-risk disease by ELN 2022 cytogenetic criteria (pending molecular profiling).

--- COMMENTS ---
The findings are diagnostic of acute myeloid leukemia. The blast percentage of 74% by aspirate differential far exceeds the 20% threshold required for AML diagnosis. Morphologic features including Auer rods, the myeloid immunophenotype, and absence of t(8;21), inv(16), and t(15;17) support the classification as AML, NOS, myeloblastic type without maturation. NPM1 and IDH1/2 are negative, and biallelic CEBPA is absent. FLT3 mutational status is critical for final ELN 2022 risk stratification and is pending; results will be communicated as an addendum upon receipt from the reference laboratory. BIOMARKER SUMMARY

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

This comprehensive NGS analysis of the diagnostic bone marrow aspirate identifies a
multi-gene mutational landscape consistent with AML arising from a clonal hematopoiesis
(CHIP) precursor state. The findings are interpreted in the context of the concurrent
diagnosis of AML, NOS (FAB M1 features) with 74% bone marrow myeloblasts and karyotype
46,XX,+8[12]/46,XX[8].

FLT3-ITD (exon 14, 33 bp insertion; VAF 48.2%; allelic ratio 0.91 — HIGH):
This is the dominant actionable finding. FLT3-ITD is present at high allelic ratio (≥0.5
by convention; this assay uses VAF-based allelic ratio = FLT3-ITD VAF / FLT3 wild-type
VAF, estimated at 0.91). High allelic ratio FLT3-ITD is independently associated with
adverse prognosis in AML, particularly in the context of NPM1 wild-type disease. Per
ELN 2022 guidelines, this patient's AML is classified as ADVERSE RISK. FLT3-ITD
constitutes a therapeutic target; FLT3 tyrosine kinase inhibitors (midostaurin in
combination with induction chemotherapy, gilteritinib in relapsed/refractory disease)
are standard-of-care options.

DNMT3A p.R882H (VAF 49.8%):
The R882H hotspot mutation in DNMT3A is the most common DNMT3A mutation in AML and in
clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). The high VAF (~50%) suggests this
mutation is present in essentially all leukemic cells, consistent with a founder clone
origin, likely predating the FLT3-ITD acquisition. DNMT3A R882H disrupts the methylation
catalytic activity of the DNMT3A homodimer via a dominant-negative mechanism, leading to
focal CpG hypomethylation at specific enhancer regions. Co-occurrence of DNMT3A R882H
with FLT3-ITD (so-called "DNMT3A/FLT3" leukemia) is a recognized biological entity and
is associated with adverse outcomes compared to FLT3-ITD without DNMT3A mutation, though
its independent prognostic impact in a fully adjusted multivariate model remains under
investigation. This mutation is not currently actionable with approved targeted agents,
but may have implications for investigational therapies targeting DNMT3A-mutant AML.

TET2 p.Q1299* (VAF 52.1%):
Loss-of-function nonsense mutation in TET2, a dioxygenase responsible for converting
5-methylcytosine to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine. VAF of 52.1% suggests this is also a
founder-level mutation, possibly co-occurring with DNMT3A R882H in the CHIP precursor
clone (DNMT3A and TET2 co-mutations are among the most common CHIP co-mutation pairs).
TET2 loss-of-function leads to impaired DNA demethylation and aberrant self-renewal of
hematopoietic stem cells. TET2 mutations are not directly targetable with approved agents,
but preclinical data suggest vitamin C (ascorbate) may partially restore TET2 enzyme
activity in TET2-mutant cells; this remains investigational.

WT1 p.R462W (VAF 31.4%):
Missense mutation in Wilms Tumor 1 (WT1), a transcription factor frequently mutated in
AML (~10-15% of cases). The sub-clonal VAF (31.4% vs. DNMT3A/TET2 at ~50%) suggests
WT1 mutation was acquired after the founder DNMT3A/TET2 clone but is present in the
majority of leukemic blasts. WT1 mutations in AML are associated with resistance to
cytarabine-based chemotherapy and have been described as MRD markers given that the
WT1 transcript level (WT1 qPCR) has been used as a disease burden surrogate. Per
concurrent WT1 quantitative PCR data, the WT1 transcript level at diagnosis was elevated;
this may serve as a complementary MRD assessment tool alongside FLT3-ITD PCR.

NRAS p.Q61H (VAF 18.7%):
Activating missense mutation in NRAS at codon 61, a well-characterized oncogenic
hot spot. The subclonal VAF (18.7%) is consistent with a late clonal event or a minor
sub-clone. RAS pathway mutations (NRAS, KRAS, NF1, PTPN11, CBL) are common in AML and
generally arise as secondary mutations contributing to proliferative signaling. Notably,
KRAS mutation is absent (consistent with the known mutual exclusivity between NRAS and
KRAS mutations in most myeloid neoplasms). This subclonal NRAS Q61H mutation could
contribute to therapeutic resistance if the sub-clone is selected under treatment
pressure. MEK/ERK pathway inhibitors may have activity against NRAS-mutant clones but
are not currently standard of care in AML.

KDM6A p.S1025fs (VAF 44.3%):
Frameshift loss-of-function mutation in KDM6A (lysine demethylase 6A, also known as
UTX), an X-linked histone H3K27 demethylase and component of the KMT2D/MLL4 complex.
KDM6A is a recurrent somatic mutation in multiple myeloid malignancies and is more
frequently mutated in females (as the second allele on Xi may already be silenced by
X-inactivation, rendering a single somatic hit sufficient for biallelic functional loss
in some cell contexts). VAF of 44.3% suggests KDM6A mutation is present in a large
clonal fraction. Loss of KDM6A alters H3K27me3 balance and impacts differentiation
block; not currently targetable with approved agents, though EZH2 inhibitors represent
a rational mechanistic approach (investigational in this context).

STAG2 p.E253* (VAF 21.6%):
Nonsense loss-of-function mutation in STAG2, encoding a cohesin complex subunit. STAG2
mutations are enriched in myeloid malignancies and are associated with aberrant DNA
replication fork stability and altered transcriptional regulation of lineage genes.
Subclonal VAF (21.6%) suggests late acquisition. Co-occurrence with DNMT3A and TET2
founders is consistent with a stepwise clonal evolution model. Not currently targetable.

BCOR p.G1522fs (VAF 14.2%):
Frameshift loss-of-function mutation in BCOR, a transcriptional repressor and component
of the PRC1.1 non-canonical polycomb complex. BCOR mutations are seen in ~4-5% of AML
cases and at higher frequency in myelodysplastic syndromes and aplastic anemia, suggesting
a role in early hematopoietic stem/progenitor cell dysregulation. The subclonal VAF
(14.2%) suggests this represents either a late clonal event or a minor competing
sub-clone. Functional impact may contribute to maintenance of leukemic stem cell
self-renewal.

Mutational Architecture and Clonal Hierarchy:
Based on VAF analysis, a plausible clonal architecture is as follows:
  - Founding clone (VAF ~50%): DNMT3A R882H, TET2 Q1299*  → likely CHIP origin
  - Dominant leukemic clone (VAF ~30-50%): FLT3-ITD, KDM6A S1025fs, WT1 R462W
    added to the DNMT3A/TET2 founder background → leukemia-initiating events
  - Subclones (VAF ~15-22%): NRAS Q61H, STAG2 E253*, BCOR G1522fs → secondary
    acquisition consistent with clonal diversification at the time of frank AML

This architecture is consistent with the concept of "CHIP-to-AML" progression in which
a founding DNMT3A/TET2 CHIP clone acquired FLT3-ITD (and KDM6A/WT1 mutations) to
undergo leukemic transformation. The FLT3-ITD allelic ratio of 0.91 is particularly
concerning and reflects either homozygosity or uniparental disomy at the FLT3 locus,
or a high-level enrichment of FLT3-ITD-bearing cells — all associated with poor outcomes
in standard cytarabine/anthracycline-based therapy without FLT3 inhibitor incorporation.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ACTIONABILITY TIERS AND THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

TIER I — STRONG CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY (FDA-approved therapy available in this
         tumor type / disease context)

  FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio)
  - Midostaurin (Rydapt): FDA-approved in combination with standard 7+3 induction
    and consolidation chemotherapy for newly diagnosed FLT3-mutated AML (based on
    RATIFY trial, Stone et al., NEJM 2017). This mutation meets criteria for
    midostaurin addition per current NCCN/ELN guidelines.
  - Gilteritinib (Xospata): FDA-approved as monotherapy for relapsed/refractory
    FLT3-mutated AML (ADMIRAL trial). Applicable if disease progresses or recurs.
  - Quizartinib (Vanflyta): FDA-approved (2023) in combination with 7+3 for newly
    diagnosed FLT3-ITD-positive AML and as maintenance after consolidation
    (QuANTUM-First trial). An alternative to midostaurin in the induction setting.
  - Clinical implications: FLT3 inhibitor therapy should be incorporated into front-
    line treatment planning. FLT3 inhibitor resistance mutations (D835, Y842, gatekeeper
    mutations) should be assessed at relapse.

  DNMT3A p.R882H
  - No FDA-approved targeted therapy specific to DNMT3A R882H at this time.
  - This mutation identifies a high-risk AML subset where allogeneic stem cell
    transplantation in first remission is strongly supported by outcome data.
  - Investigational: SY-2101 (oral azacitidine), CC-486, and hypomethylating agents
    may have mechanistic rationale; enrollment in clinical trials may be appropriate.

TIER II — POTENTIAL ACTIONABILITY (clinical evidence in investigational setting,
          or approved therapy in another tumor type with biological rationale)

  TET2 p.Q1299* (loss of function)
  - Investigational: High-dose ascorbate (vitamin C) to restore TET2 enzymatic
    activity (preclinical rationale; no approved indication).
  - HMAs (azacitidine, decitabine) have preclinical and clinical rationale in
    TET2-mutant myeloid disease; used in maintenance settings.

  WT1 p.R462W
  - WT1-directed immunotherapy (WT1 peptide vaccine, WT1-specific T-cell therapy)
    under investigation in AML; not currently standard of care.
  - Utility as MRD biomarker: WT1 qPCR may complement FLT3-ITD PCR for MRD
    monitoring given subclonal heterogeneity.

  NRAS p.Q61H (subclonal)
  - MEK inhibitors (trametinib) have preclinical activity in NRAS-mutant AML;
    not FDA-approved for AML. Clinical trial consideration if NRAS clone expands
    at relapse.

  KDM6A p.S1025fs
  - EZH2 inhibitors (tazemetostat) have mechanistic rationale in KDM6A-deficient
    malignancies; investigational in AML.

TIER III — UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE / INFORMATIONAL ONLY

  STAG2 p.E253* — not currently targetable; informational for risk stratification
  BCOR p.G1522fs — not currently targetable; informational

TIER IV — NOT DETECTED, CLINICALLY RELEVANT NEGATIVE RESULTS

  NPM1 wild-type: Absence of NPM1 mutation in the setting of FLT3-ITD upgrades ELN
  risk from intermediate to ADVERSE. NPM1 mutation absence eliminates eligibility
  for NPM1-directed MRD strategies as the sole marker; FLT3-ITD and WT1 PCR serve
  as alternative MRD modalities.

  IDH1/IDH2 wild-type: Patient is not a candidate for ivosidenib (IDH1), enasidenib
  or olutasidenib (IDH2) at this time.

  TP53 wild-type: Absence of TP53 mutation is favorable; etoposide/decitabine
  combinations (common in TP53-mutant AML) are not specifically indicated.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LIMITATIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. This assay cannot detect all possible mutations in the 468 genes tested; deep
   intronic variants, large structural rearrangements outside fusion panel coverage,
   and variants in genomic regions of low mappability may not be detected.
2. VAF-based clonal hierarchy is an approximation; tumor heterogeneity, aneuploidy,
   and LOH can affect VAF interpretation.
3. Constitutional (germline) variants cannot be fully excluded without matched normal
   tissue sequencing. DNMT3A R882H and TET2 Q1299* at high VAF may represent germline
   predisposition variants; germline testing should be considered per institutional
   policy, particularly given the probable CHIP-origin leukemia scenario.
4. FLT3-ITD allelic ratio by NGS is calculated from VAF data and may differ modestly
   from gel-based PCR allelic ratio methodology; both methods are acceptable per ELN
   2022 guidance.
5. This report reflects findings at the time of specimen analysis. Tumor evolution,
   clonal selection under therapy, and emergence of resistance mutations are expected
   and should be re-evaluated at relapse.

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS -- MORPHOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bone Marrow Biopsy (H&E and Giemsa stains, bilateral cores):
The biopsy cores are markedly HYPOCELLULAR. Estimated overall marrow cellularity is 10-15% (reference range for age: approximately 50-60%). The adipocytes are prominent and constitute the majority of the marrow space. The residual hematopoietic elements are distributed in small, loosely clustered foci scattered throughout the adipose tissue. No sheets, clusters, or aggregates of blasts are identified. There is no evidence of residual leukemic infiltrate on core biopsy.

The residual hematopoietic islands, while small in number, demonstrate evidence of trilineage hematopoiesis:
- Erythropoiesis: Small erythroid islands are present. Erythroid precursors appear morphologically normal without dysplastic features. Normoblastic maturation is appreciated.
- Granulopoiesis: Scattered myeloid precursors are seen with apparent left-shift consistent with early marrow regeneration post-chemotherapy. No Auer rods identified. No excess blasts morphologically.
- Megakaryopoiesis: Occasional megakaryocytes are identified, including forms with normal polylobated nuclei. No megakaryocyte clustering or dysplastic forms (hypogranulation, hypolobation) are identified beyond what is expected in a regenerating post-chemotherapy marrow.

Blast Estimation (morphology): Myeloblasts estimated at less than 3% of nucleated marrow cells by morphologic assessment on H&E and touch imprint preparations. No Auer rods identified in the current specimen.

Reticulin Stain: Grade 0-1 fibrosis (focal fine reticulin fibers without collagen deposition). No significant increase in reticulin compared to the diagnostic specimen.

No granulomas, no lymphoid aggregates, no plasma cell excess, no evidence of myeloid sarcoma or extramedullary extension.

Impression (Morphology): Markedly hypocellular marrow consistent with post-induction aplasia/hypoplasia with early trilineage regeneration. No morphologic evidence of residual acute myeloid leukemia.
